text: Good morning to one and all. We from the Department of Urology at Apple Hospital, Chennai, are presenting a robotic heminephrectomy for a non-functioning moiety in a household kidney. A thirty-two-year-old female presented with deaf-loin pain for six months. Her CT revealed a left-sided non-functioning moiety in a household kidney.
The post-op test as shown in the figure.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and mobilizes the colon from the lateral abdominal wall, tracing along the wall. The ureter is further mobilized and used as a landmark to trace the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety.

The surgeon meticulously mobilizes the colon near the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety, reaching the upper pole. The lower pole of the non-functioning moiety is then dissected, and an aberrant vessel at the lower pole is clipped.
The video shows the anatomical structures including the ureter, the lower pole artery, and the clearly demarcated lower pole of the kidney.
The surgeon performs medial dissection at the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety, identifying and looping the left renal vein.
The surgeon carefully dissects the renal artery located behind the renal vein, and clips the artery, ensuring precision during this critical step.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the upper pole of the kidney, revealing the psoas muscle posteriorly after controlling the vessels.
The surgeon mobilizes the kidney, fully freeing it from surrounding tissues. The non-functioning moiety is carefully dismembered from the normal kidney, separating the two structures.

The surgeon opens the collecting system of the normal kidney, placing a stay suture at the corner of the incision to provide traction for continued dismemberment of the non-functioning moiety beyond the isthmus.
The surgeon completes the dismemberment of the non-functional kidney moiety, separating it entirely from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon cuts the ureter and performs a pyelography on the normal kidney. Continuous sutures are applied, and the specimen is placed into a bag for removal.
